Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (29) - BeautifulSoup

We're heading into the home stretch of the Ironman contest, so this post covers a really common package: BeautifulSoup. It's one of the most-used tools for web scraping in Python, so let's go learn how to use BeautifulSoup.
Python gets used a lot for web scraping, and the package most people reach for is BeautifulSoup. So... what exactly is BeautifulSoup?
Let me just borrow Wikipedia's description here:

Beautiful Soup is a Python package for parsing HTML and XML documents, including ones with malformed markup like unclosed tags (these are often called "tag soup"). It creates a parse tree for the page that can be used to extract data, which is super handy for web scraping.
From that we can roughly gather that BeautifulSoup mainly parses the HTML files we download, and using it makes grabbing specific chunks of data way easier.
Oh, by the way — if you've ever looked up BeautifulSoup, you'll have noticed its cover image is a medieval-style picture, and a pretty distinctive one at that:

The name BeautifulSoup comes from a poem in Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, which is why some translated docs even call it the "Alice document." That's also why the package's cover image is always medieval-style — after all, Alice in Wonderland is set in medieval Europe (if I remember right).
That was just a little fun aside about BeautifulSoup. Now let's get on with installing it!

Installing BeautifulSoup is a bit different from the Requests package — you don't have to worry about the package name being different on Python2 versus Python3, because the name stays the same during installation.
Once it's installed, you can import the BeautifulSoup package into your Python project. But here's a quirky thing — when you install BeautifulSoup, the module name is actually
, so we usually rename it when importing:
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup # this is definitely NOT the Bootstrap4 you're thinking of
here is absolutely NOT Bootstrap4, it's BeautifulSoup4! (Important.)
Next you can try pasting in the sample code the official docs provide — this one's also known as the

from bs4 import BeautifulSoup html_doc = """ The Dormouse's story

The Dormouse's story

Once upon a time there were three little sisters; and their names wereElsie,Lacie andTillie;and they lived at the bottom of a well.

...

"""

The Dormouse's story

Once upon a time there were three little sisters; and their names wereElsie,Lacie andTillie;and they lived at the bottom of a well.

...

You can see the HTML above is minified, which makes it really hard to read. But BeautifulSoup can turn that compressed HTML structure into properly indented, standard HTML — the kind of format a frontend engineer can actually read. And the usage is dead simple, you just write this:

from bs4 import BeautifulSoup html_doc = """ The Dormouse's story

The Dormouse's story

Once upon a time there were three little sisters; and their names wereElsie,Lacie andTillie;and they lived at the bottom of a well.

...

""" soup = BeautifulSoup(html_doc, 'html.parser') print(soup.prettify())
Here you can see one of the lines is
. The second argument is actually telling BeautifulSoup which parser to use for parsing the HTML. The official site explains which parsers it recommends, and the official suggestion is to use the
parser. If you want to use that one, you'll need to install it separately:
Once it's installed, remember to switch the parser:
outputs some very standard, nicely formatted HTML. The terminal might still feel hard to read, though, so you can use the file-writing trick we learned earlier to generate an HTML file:

from bs4 import BeautifulSoup html_doc = """ The Dormouse's story

The Dormouse's story

Once upon a time there were three little sisters; and their names wereElsie,Lacie andTillie;and they lived at the bottom of a well.

...

""" soup = BeautifulSoup(html_doc, 'lxml') f = open('index.html', 'w') f.write(soup.prettify()) f.close()
Then when you open index.html it'll be much easier to read — after all, you're a frontend engineer, right?

So what else does BeautifulSoup have? Let's keep digging into it!
BeautifulSoup gives you a ton of methods for searching HTML tags, so let's try each one out.
is for searching multiple HTML elements:
print(soup.find_all('a')) # [Elsie, Lacie, Tillie]
can also search by an HTML element's attributes:
Since there's a way to search by HTML element, there's naturally a way to search HTML elements by CSS selector too. First, searching by ID:
And another way is searching by class:

print(soup.select('.story')) # [

Once upon a time there were three little sisters; and their names wereElsie,Lacie andTillie;and they lived at the bottom of a well.

,

...

]
can also search directly by HTML tag:
, but it mainly returns a single element that matches the condition:
— it only returns the first matching element:

print(soup.select('.story')) # print(soup.select_one('.story')) #

Once upon a time there were three little sisters; and their names wereElsie,Lacie andTillie;and they lived at the bottom of a well.

Side note — if you use the
method, it's fine even when the attribute doesn't exist:
print(soup.find_all('a')[0].get('title', "I don't exist")) # I don't exist
I've only listed the BeautifulSoup methods I use most here. If you want the more detailed stuff, I'd recommend checking the official docs.
